text: The video displays images of a herniation in the right inguinal canal, visibly involving the bladder.
Starting the case, I had the joint assistance of a urologist who was available in case we got into the bladder, which we expected with this anatomy.
The surgeon begins the robotic dissection for the inguinal hernia repair, starting slightly above the previous mesh placement to access untouched anatomical structures and avoid heavy scarring.
The surgeon approaches the hernia laterally, avoiding the cord structures and epigastric vessels. The mesh is visibly adhered to the peritoneum and surrounding structures.
The video shows the site of reherniation occurring in the inferior and medial positions relative to the mesh, which remains intact.

The surgeon, with the assistance of a urologist, uses a cystoscope to identify the herniated portion of the bladder. Significant traction and counter-traction are applied to reduce the bladder from the hernia sac. The robotic camera and arms facilitate the process, and ICG is administered to identify the epigastric vessels. The bladder is successfully reduced and transected from the hernia sac, as verified by cystoscopy.

The anatomical defect and direct space on the patient's right side are shown. A thicker 3D Max right-sided inguinal mesh is placed over the defect. The mesh is affixed in two or three spots using absorbable sutures, securing it at the pubic tubercle and anteriorly on either side of the epigastric vessels to prevent migration.

The surgeon closes the peritoneum using a 3-0 V-lock suture, ensuring it completely covers the mesh. The mesh is securely positioned, and the closure provides an additional layer of separation from the intestines. The gas is then aspirated from the peritoneal cavity.
And the patient did very well, was discharged home postoperative day zero with a Foley catheter to remain in place for ten days.
